title: Executive Dysfunction Explained: The 7 Functions and ADHD
description:
Executive dysfunction is not laziness, bad attitude, or lack of effort. In this video, we break down all 7 executive functions, explain exactly what is happening in the ADHD brain for each one, and walk through why standard advice fails and what actually helps. If you have ever stared at a task for an hour and still could not start, this video is for you.

Chapters:

chapters.title: You Are Not Lazy
chapters.voiceover: It is Tuesday morning. The email has been sitting open in your browser for forty minutes. You have read the first line six times. You know it is not hard. You know it will take maybe four minutes to reply. And yet here you are, doing absolutely nothing, while a quiet voice in the back of your head calls you useless. That voice is wrong. And this video is going to prove it. What you are experiencing has a name. It is called executive dysfunction, and it is one of the most misunderstood features of the ADHD brain. Not a personality flaw. Not a moral failing. A neurological difference in how your brain regulates and initiates behavior. Today we are going to walk through all seven executive functions, what each one actually does, what is happening in your brain when ADHD disrupts it, and what kinds of strategies actually match the real problem. Not generic productivity advice. Not 'just break it into smaller steps.' Real, neuroscience-grounded explanations. By the end of this video you will understand your own brain in a way that most people, including a lot of clinicians, honestly do not. So let us start from the beginning.

chapters.title: What Executive Function Actually Is
chapters.voiceover: The term executive function was coined by neuropsychologist Alexander Luria in the mid-twentieth century, and it refers to a set of mental processes that sit at the very top of your cognitive hierarchy. Think of them as the brain's management system. They do not do the work directly. They decide what work gets done, in what order, with how much effort, and when to stop. These functions are primarily coordinated by the prefrontal cortex, the large frontal region of the brain that is disproportionately affected in ADHD. Here is the key neurological fact: in ADHD, the prefrontal cortex is underactivated, not because it is damaged, but because it is not receiving sufficient dopamine and norepinephrine signals to function at full capacity. Dopamine in this context is not just the pleasure chemical the internet loves to talk about. It is a critical signal that tells your prefrontal cortex that something matters, that it is worth sustained effort. Without adequate dopamine tone, the prefrontal cortex essentially idles. And when your management system idles, seven very specific things start to break down. Researchers including Russell Barkley have identified these seven functions as the core of what ADHD disrupts. We are going to go through each one. Understanding them individually is what separates 'I am broken' from 'I know exactly what is happening and why.'

chapters.title: Functions 1 and 2: Working Memory and Inhibition
chapters.voiceover: Function one is working memory. This is your brain's mental whiteboard, the place where you hold information actively while you use it. You walk into a room to get something. By the time you get there, it is gone. You start a sentence and lose the point mid-way through. You forget what you just read four seconds ago. This is not forgetfulness in the traditional sense. It is a working memory system that drops information faster than it can be used. In the ADHD brain, reduced dopamine signaling in the prefrontal cortex means the whiteboard essentially keeps erasing itself. The practical fix that actually works: externalize everything. The goal is not to remember harder. The goal is to never rely on memory at all. Write the thing the second you think it. Say it out loud so you hear it twice. Put the object where the action is. If you need to take your medication with breakfast, put the bottle next to the coffee machine. Memory lives in the environment, not in the brain. Function two is inhibition, and this one is at the absolute core of ADHD. Inhibition is your ability to pause before acting, to suppress an impulse or an irrelevant thought long enough to make a deliberate choice. Low inhibitory control does not mean you have no self-control. It means the brake system activates too slowly. You say the thing before you thought it through. You click away from the task. You interrupt without meaning to. The gap between impulse and action, where everyone else makes decisions, is just narrower for ADHD brains. This is structural. This is dopamine. This is not who you are.

chapters.title: Functions 3, 4 and 5: Emotional Regulation, Shifting and Planning
chapters.voiceover: Function three is emotional regulation, and this one is criminally underrepresented in most ADHD conversations. The ADHD brain does not just have trouble regulating attention. It has trouble regulating emotional intensity. Rejection, frustration, excitement, even a mildly harsh email can hit with a force that feels completely disproportionate. This is called emotional impulsivity, and it connects directly to inhibition. When the braking system is slow, emotions do not get modulated before they land. They arrive at full volume. This is why ADHD burnout happens. The emotional load is just higher, constantly. Knowing this does not make it disappear, but it does mean you can stop spending energy wondering why you react so strongly and start spending it on regulation tools, physical ones: slow exhale before responding, cold water, movement to discharge the spike. Function four is cognitive flexibility, or shifting. This is your ability to transition between tasks, ideas, or mental sets. When this is impaired, transitions feel like being wrenched out of something. Switching from work to a meeting, from one tab to another, from a task you chose to a task someone asks you to do, all of these create real neurological friction. It is not stubbornness. The brain is mid-process and lacks the dopamine signal to smoothly disengage. Transition cues, small rituals that signal a shift is coming, help dramatically. A five-minute timer. Closing one physical thing before opening another. Function five is planning and organization, the ability to look at a future goal and construct the steps backward to now. For ADHD brains, the future is neurologically abstract. If it is not happening right now, it barely registers as real. This is why deadlines feel urgent only when they are immediate. It is not poor prioritization. It is a time perception deficit baked into the neurology.

chapters.title: Functions 6 and 7: Self-Monitoring and Task Initiation
chapters.voiceover: Function six is self-monitoring, your brain's ability to observe itself in real time, to notice that you have drifted off task, that your tone shifted, that the plan is not working and needs adjusting. In ADHD, this metacognitive loop is delayed. You do not realize you have been scrolling for twenty minutes until you are thirty minutes in. You do not notice the meeting is going sideways until it already has. This is not obliviousness. It is a monitoring system that checks in less frequently. What helps: external checkpoints. Set a recurring timer that simply asks the question: what am I doing right now, is it what I intended? That external prompt does the job the internal system is not doing automatically. Function seven is task initiation, and this might be the one that causes the most shame. Because it looks, from the outside and from the inside, like you are just not doing it. But initiation is a completely separate function from motivation. You can fully want to do something, know you need to do it, feel the pressure of not doing it, and still be completely unable to start. Why? Because in the ADHD brain, the dopamine signal required to fire up the prefrontal cortex and begin sustained action is not generated by importance or intention. It is generated by novelty, urgency, interest, or challenge. This is Barkley's framework: ADHD brains run on a now-or-not-now system. If the task does not trigger a dopamine response, the start signal simply does not fire. The fix is not discipline. The fix is manufacturing the condition that generates the signal. Work somewhere new. Put on specific music only used for this task. Tell someone what you are about to do out loud. Race a timer for just ten minutes. You are not cheating. You are giving your brain the input it actually needs to function.

chapters.title: What This All Means for You
chapters.voiceover: Here is what we just covered. Seven executive functions. Working memory, the whiteboard that keeps erasing. Inhibition, the brake that activates too slowly. Emotional regulation, the volume dial that goes to ten without warning. Cognitive flexibility, the painful friction of transitions. Planning, the future that feels neurologically invisible. Self-monitoring, the check-in that does not come automatically. And task initiation, the start signal that needs the right input to fire. Every single one of these is coordinated by the same prefrontal dopamine system that ADHD disrupts. Which means when you struggle with all of these at once, it is not because you have seven separate problems. It is because one underlying neurological difference ripples across seven domains of function. That is not seven reasons to feel bad about yourself. That is one explanation. And one explanation is something you can actually work with. The strategies that help are not the ones that ask you to be more disciplined. They are the ones that reshape your environment to compensate for what the brain is not doing automatically. Externalize memory. Manufacture initiation triggers. Build in transition cues. Create external monitoring checkpoints. Work with the dopamine system, not against it. You have probably spent years trying to fix your character. This video is an invitation to stop doing that and start understanding your neurology instead. Because you were never the problem. Your brain just needed a different manual. If this helped, follow Bloom Focus for daily ADHD content that is always grounded in real science.
